body_txt: We're out of food . Now what? That guy in the bucket hat is looking pretty tasty . Why don't we Just Eat Gilligan — literally? This trope is the ugly side of exploration. The characters are out of food and resort to cannibalism not because they like it , but because they have no other choice. It's that or starve to death . Maybe they eat people who've already died, maybe they resort to murder. Named for the famous example of the Donner Party , who ate the bodies of their comrades who had died of hunger in order to survive the harsh winter of 1846-47, and for the song "Ain't No Party Like an S Club Party" by S Club 7 . Note that, in a real-life survival situation, this is often of limited help. The meat of a starving human, by definition, does not contain the necessary nutrients to support a starving human. (See fat starvation. ) Fat may still be found in certain organs and bone marrow, and of course in cases without a prolonged fast a body would still have some fat in it. Also worth noting is that digesting meat requires certain vitamins that can't be replenished through meat alone, potentially leading to death by malnutrition. It's definitely not a long-term solution even if plenty of corpses are available, though it might just stave off starvation for long enough for rescue to hopefully arrive. Compare Emergency Food Supply Animal , Reduced to Ratburgers , Too Desperate to Be Picky , Eating Shoes , Cold Equation . Contrast I'm a Humanitarian , where the character eats their fellow human out of enjoyment. When animals or monsters eat their own kind, thus demonstrating they're ravenous and/or vicious, that's Monstrous Cannibalism .
